turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: I've been having some issues with my stomach. I've had crampy pain in my lower abdomen, mostly on the left side, and I've also been going to the bathroom a lot more than usual - 8 or 10 times a day, which is way out of the norm for me. It's really disrupting my work.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: It's not just uncomfortable, it's affecting my job. I'm a software engineer and I've been working 60-hour weeks, trying to meet deadlines, but with this diarrhea and pain, I'm constantly worried about having an accident at work or missing meetings because of bathroom breaks.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: I've also been feeling really fatigued lately, and it's hard for me to focus at work because of the urgency I feel when I need to go to the bathroom. Sometimes I have bloody stools with mucus, which is pretty scary.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: No, I don't usually feel hot or sweaty, and I haven't felt like I'm going to pass out. But, sometimes I get a low-grade fever, just a slight temperature, around 38 degrees Celsius, a few times a week.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: The pain usually stays mostly on the left side of my lower abdomen, but sometimes it can radiate a bit to my back, just above the waistline. It's not always consistent, though - some days it feels like it's stuck in one spot, and other days it moves around a bit.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: No, just my stomach and lower abdomen, really. It's been a challenge to manage everything with this going on.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: No, I haven't noticed any swelling in my body, no. To be honest, I've been so focused on managing my diarrhea and pain that I haven't really paid attention to other areas of my body, but nothing comes to mind right now.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't really felt nauseous, but taking Imodium has helped with the discomfort and slowed down my bowel movements a bit. But even with that, I still experience some urgency and loose stools, which is frustrating because it feels like nothing's working for long.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: My energy levels have really taken a hit. I feel tired all the time, and it's hard to get through my workday without feeling exhausted. As for my appetite, it's been irregular because of my busy schedule at work, but even when I do eat, I don't feel like I'm retaining much nutrition. I've noticed some unintentional weight loss over the past few weeks, which is concerning me a lot.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: I've actually had low-grade fevers, not exactly fevers that would wake me up at night, but a slight temperature around 38 degrees Celsius, just a few times per week. And, to be honest, I have woken up with nocturnal diarrhea 2 or 3 times a night, which makes it really hard for me to get a good night's sleep.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: I had an appendectomy when I was 16 years old, but that was a long time ago and it didn't cause any complications. Other than that, I'm not aware of any other significant medical conditions in my family or myself.

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: I've actually been self-medicating with loperamide for diarrhea for the past 2 weeks because I couldn't get an appointment with my doctor and was desperate for some relief. The Imodium has helped slow down my bowel movements a bit, but it's not really addressing the underlying issue, and I'm still experiencing loose stools and urgency.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: To be honest, my eating habits have been pretty irregular lately due to my long work hours. I usually grab takeout or fast food for lunch because it's quick and convenient, but I try to cook healthy meals at home when I can. It's not always easy, though - sometimes I'll go days without cooking a proper meal, especially on nights when I'm working late.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: Honestly, it's been tough to find time for exercise lately. With 60-hour work weeks and irregular eating habits, I've just been trying to survive each day. When I do have some downtime, I try to squeeze in a short walk around my apartment complex or some light stretching, but that's about it. Exercise hasn't been a priority when I'm struggling with these symptoms.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: Yes, my older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. That was a few years ago, and she's been managing it since then, going through various treatments and adjusting her diet to cope with the condition. It was really tough for our family when we first found out, but she's doing okay now, and we're trying to stay supportive.

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: It's hard not to think about it, to be honest. When my sister got diagnosed with Crohn's, I was really scared. Now, dealing with these symptoms myself, it's natural for me to wonder if I might have something similar going on. But at the same time, I've been trying to focus on managing this and getting some answers from a doctor, rather than jumping to conclusions or worrying too much about it.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: Yes, I do drink alcohol. Usually 2-3 beers on weeknights, but over the past month, I've found myself drinking more - sometimes up to 4 or 5 beers a night because of the stress from my symptoms and work. It's been helping me relax for a bit, but I know it's not addressing the root cause of my issues.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, I don't smoke.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: Honestly, I'm worried that I might be doing more harm than good with my Imodium use. I feel like it's just masking the symptoms and not addressing the underlying issue. And to be honest, sometimes when I drink a bit too much, I think it might make things worse, but at the same time, it does help me relax a little.

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: To be honest, I'm really scared about what might be going on with my body. The bloody stools and mucus are what keep me up at night, worrying that it's something serious like Crohn's or something even worse. I know my sister had a tough time dealing with her diagnosis, and now seeing these symptoms in myself... it's just really frightening for me.
